speaker: interviewer
text: --before we take a break?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am--

speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about your son, Pavel, or Paul, as he's known here. The draft came about. And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was drafted. And he was-- he didn't speak too well English, because it was really not even a year-- a year after we came. And he was placed for his basic training in Fort Bliss. And then they moved him to El Paso where he was enrolled Air Force school for electronics.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- you know, that when he had to fill out the form, so his English was not too good. So he made only up to 60 points. But the practical, where he had to do the, you know, the practical thing with the electronic, he was 100%. So there were 30 students. And only, I think so, under 10 made it. So he was in the Air Force school for electronics in El Paso.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he served there schooling for two years. Then he was placed in Frankfort, because he was trained for repairing intercontinental missiles. Missiles, I would say. So those were placed not far away from the Czech borders, stationed, you know, in-- in Frankfort, and that was his job, monitoring the Russian block. Sundays, what he told us, the American soldiers went to Prague. But they wouldn't let him, because he was born in Czechoslovakia. So anyway, he served four years there, two years there, two years at school here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after, when he was released, they offered him jobs in all kinds of-- he-- they offered him-- I don't remember what companies. And he always asked, do-- do you have something in San Francisco? They said, no, it's not in San Francisco. Then I don't take it.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came home, discharged, no job. But we had already, at this time, Breder's Villa. So we opened another one in Daly City for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's a Breder's Villa?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Breder's Villa is a retirement home which we immigrants were just Czechoslovakia founded. But it's a long story. First of all, my employment. Yes? When we came, like I told you, Fred had already, after two days, had a job in a bakery. And I didn't have what to do, because I didn't have to babysit my children. They're grown ups. So I said I have to do something, at least, to earn money now for pay the rent and provide the food. And we can save a little bit. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to the Jewish Family Service. And they found me a job cleaning houses. Every day, another house I cleaned, day by day. From morning to evening, I went cleaning. It was a very hard job, but I did it. Because I-- I got paid $1 an hour and no food. I have to bring my lunch.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, I got one job in a small restaurant. And I worked there a month. And I said, I'm not going to work for one-- I rebelled-- I am not going to work for one hour cleaning, and cooking, and doing this job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, she said. Oh. What more do you want? And I said, I want $2.50. She said $2.50? You'll never get a job. You don't know English. I said, fine. So I stayed at that home. I had what to eat. I have a roof for my head. I'm not going to work-- and hard work eight hours. I got it. And I worked there eight months. But I learned a trade.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw when she, this woman, which was a very, very plain, primitive person, can make it, I would with my little finger. But we needed money for a down payment. So we had saved already in these eight months, I think it's over $1,000. Because we were working, both. Then I asked my sister for a loan. I found a place on the 37th Avenue with six people. It was a house which had eight bedrooms. And so we could live there, didn't have to pay rent. And I could manage and pay off the mortgage. And Fred is working, and one salary. And we have what to eat there. And one salary, we can save.

speaker: interviewer
text: So exactly we did it. But I didn't know fluently to talk to the real estate. I was looking in the Jewish papers, in the bulletin, and then in the And I found a place in the which was the certain address, went to see it. The price was not so bad. Then I got the mortgage from my sister, $1,500.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we needed 5% for a down payment. And the real estate, I asked if they have someone who speak a Slavic language. Oh, yes. Anita speaks fluently Russian. I said, [NON-ENGLISH], good. And with Anita, but she spoke Yiddish, too. What I couldn't say in Russian, I said in Yiddish. And we did it. But, but what happened, you know, the woman who sold the house was ready to retire. She was already over 70. And she wanted very badly to sell this house, so we got the price down, what she asked for. And we put the down payment. But when we moved in, in the morning, but did it had already a license, too. But I had to get own license. But I had practice. I got a license. In the morning, when I went down to the kitchen, I turned on the-- the lights. It was covered with cockroaches, everything the floor, every-- the stove. I started screaming. Fred was at work. Or, he was sleeping already. I called Anita. And I told her what did she do to me. And she said, Linda, calm down. [NON-ENGLISH], she told me. Calm down. You have the title company $1,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: The former owner has to pay for it to clean up the house. And then they came, you know? The exterminator came, and they cleaned house.

speaker: interviewee
text: But Fred worked in the bakery at night, and it was carpeted. At daytime, we tore out the carpet for every room. It was hardwood flooring. And we needed furniture. We went to [? McAlister. ?] I don't know if you remember. They were all junk stores. We bought. When we got-- and the Jewish Family Service made the referrals. People-- when people get old, they get back to their native language. And my English was not so good, still. So I had people, immigrants mostly, Germans who were in [? Shanghai. ?] No problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean his clients?

speaker: interviewee
text: As clients, sure. And it was licensed only for six. But, you know, I couldn't keep more. But I kept eight, because I wanted to make money. And morning, when Fred came, and I know inspector is coming, he had to drive the two extra people around San Francisco. I don't tell-- I tell you, we went through it. But we made it. Five years we run the place. But we were so successful, because I cooked good. I handled the people good. You know, the house was spic and span clean, like my house. It was my house. We lived there, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have to ask you this. And I wanted to ask you before. You were so young when you left home and went to Auschwitz. Where did you learn to cook?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- ask Fred. You know, when we married, on his stomach. When we married, I didn't know that a chicken-- I bought a chicken with feathers. I know they have to be cleaned. I throw them in the hot water. I took off thing, but I didn't know they have to be open.

speaker: interviewer
text: I threw the whole chicken. I cooked everything together. I learned on him. I didn't know how to-- you know, I told you, such a 14-year-old girl. What, what? My mind is all on girlfriends. But anyway, I learned everything, everything, to clean, to cook, to handle people, everything very fast. When I came here, I was 42 years old. So you had this Breder's Villa on 37th Avenue.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was only Breder's.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Breder's.

speaker: interviewee
text: After five years, when we saved money and I saw I have a chance to make a little bit more, to put through my children through college and for them to have a better [INAUDIBLE], better life than we did. So we did everything. So I said, you know what? We are going to start to see to build a bigger place. We bought a lot on the 42nd Avenue. There were two lots. And we had a little money, so we put a down payment. And we the-- we paid it. But we got an architect in 1968. But we couldn't-- we got-- we got a permit to build a brand new retirement home for 22 people, 22 rooms. But we couldn't get a loan, because we didn't have more money. And you know, we didn't have any savings, nothing. Because everything we put in on this lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we sold the lot. And I went to visit one retirement home-- a Jewish girl run it from Germany-- on Anza Street. She bought two houses. And she converted the apartment building, which was apartment building, one house, six apartments, for a retirement home. And she-- like a guest house. So I got an idea. So I said, you know what? We can't get a loan, but a loan for an existing building we would get. We sold the property, so we'll have a down payment for the house. So we bought the 10th Avenue house. And from whatever we had paid off from the lot on the 42nd, we put the down payment. And then we converted it to a retirement home, one, by one, by one.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have-- you know, when we put the down payment, we didn't have one penny saved. But I said, when we rent the house, we will have. And that happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: We always risked. And we always said, what can you risk more? We will have [INAUDIBLE] the $40 that we came with. What? So how long did you have the villa?

speaker: interviewee
text: We still have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Oh, you still have it. So he's working there actively now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My son-in-law runs the place. And then, like I said, Paul, we had another place in Daly City. But we converted it back to an apartment, because he didn't want to do that. He has his own business, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to go, just for a moment, and talk about something that might have occurred. You talked about you testified against some people. You were a witness in some trials. And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first trial it was against Nazi criminals, I would say, war criminals, was in Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- I testified already-- I sent my testimony by mail to the Austrian courts in 1964. And then, in--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you asked? Who asked you to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, you know, the Austrian Justice Department. Because these times, Simon Wiesenthal had a small office in Vienna. In the '60s, he started with his daughter. And Erich Kulka, who was-- who just died recently, a good friend and fellow survivor, he obtained photographs from Prague, from the Prague Jewish community. He was living in Prague at this time. And I-- he brought me photographs to identify from the photographs, from the [INAUDIBLE]. And I did. But the main thing how I got as a witness in Vienna-- our good friends, Fred's and mine, two our friends who were three years in the camp, both, like we, Fred and me. And their wives were also three years in Auschwitz, from Presov. And they were living in Presov, immigrated in 1965 to Israel. But they still lived there. They went in and out to Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when they came to Vienna, they went to a restaurant. And somebody came to them and said, in German, Alex, you know, [GERMAN]. Oh, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? What are you doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he turned around. It was the SS guard from Auschwitz, owning this huge, beautiful restaurant. He went to Simon Wiesenthal. And he reported them. And he said, also, survivors from Auschwitz mentioned my name, Fred's name. And so when we are--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in Czechoslovakia at that time, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Bratislava. Half an hour from Vienna, we were living. And so they called me, but they called witnesses from the registry from all over the world. So in 1972, we were already in America. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was the person who you were called for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hans Wunsch and Otto Graff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Both at the same time?

speaker: interviewee
text: They both were guards-- SS guards in the working place where I was three years. And I went to the trial from America already. And at the trial, you know, when I was on the witness stand, the judge asked me, do you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you point at the man? Because I told my story where I know him, the blah, blah, blah. And I said, yes. Which man are we talking about now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both were the same time there, Hans Wunsch and Otto Graff. And I point at them, yeah. And then the judge-- because I was s speaking in German at the trial. They didn't need any translator. And pointed to both of those SS and asked them, do you know? And they said, yeah, yeah, with a heavy Austrian-- I mean Viennese [INAUDIBLE]. And they looked at me and smiled, like nothing happened. Indeed, they were set free. Nothing happened to them, both, both killers and sadists. But I think so, Austria is very anti-Semitic, even now, always was. They didn't want the trial, first of all. And they didn't want to sentence for anybody. Otherwise, they would have to take to trial the whole population. Otto Graff, the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these both were witnesses at the same trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the crime for which they were actually--

speaker: interviewee
text: The crime--

speaker: interviewer
text: --being tried?

speaker: interviewee
text: The crime what they did is they were sadists. They were beating mainly men, not us women. And the main thing what they were sentenced both, when I told the story after the uprising, what I saw, the killing. You had to tell them exactly the same was in Wuppertal, when, and if you saw him killing. Otherwise, it didn't work at all. Even though I told them, and other witnesses too, it didn't mean a thing to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what was your testimony? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the testimony when-- when, you know, in 1944, when the uprising was, beside others, was in the crematorium. And the sonderkommando escaped and were hiding in the clothing in the barracks where I was assigned to. So they found them. And they dragged them through the lagerstrasse, and kept kicking them. And they killed them. So that was a crime which I saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you cross-examined as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No cross-examination?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Very, very slightly. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the judge?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a jury?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was not in Germany. In Austria, it's not a jury trial. It's like a Roman trial. You have only three judges, no jury. In Germany, the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he found guilty or innocent?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were freed and innocent. Because if they were acquitted, you know, it's different. But they were set free.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt that it was my trial. They judged me, and not them. I was guilty of being in camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what happened to these two men?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They have a-- have a beautiful life, families. And they-- they stole so much money for their lifetime for their families. Because they were there at the source so many years.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying that one of the trials was in Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of that particular person was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like you would put over us witnesses cold water.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what was his name, the gentleman who was being tried in Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wunsch, Hans Wunsch and Otto Graff.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then you said something about Wuppertal.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was a different trial in Germany, in West Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1987. 1987 was different. I was, like I said here, at the consulate. I recognized them and was asked to be a witness at the trial. And I agreed. And we went there with my husband and my daughter, Dasha, to the trial.

speaker: interviewer
text: And of the name of that person again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gottfried Weiser.

speaker: interviewer
text: Weiser.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you stay during the trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in a hotel in Wuppertal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you sworn to secrecy? Or did you have free--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You could walk around town?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- I got assigned a German lawyer in-- in Wuppertal, who was next to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they prepare you for the case?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't prepare me at all for the case. It just was my memory there. Absolutely, no. They didn't have nothing to say. Because the only one who had to say something was me, because I was there where the crime occurred.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are other witnesses besides you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but not at the same time and the same day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long--

speaker: interviewee
text: The one day-- I had only one day.

speaker: interviewer
text: One day. And who paid for your trip over there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. I mean, the German Justice Department. There I got-- I got the tickets to fly, tickets here, at the consulate. And the rest of it, it was paid all by them, the hotel, the stay, the food. And the first thing what the judge did when I came to the trial was testing me, four hours. They had a map of Auschwitz blown up like this window. And I had to point out directly where I came, where I was, where I saw exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what? They had-- I corrected them. Because the entrance, they had in a different way. Four hours, they tested if-- me if I am real, if it's not false witness. Four hours. What was the outcome of this trial?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was sentenced-- well, later, he was sentenced. He was found guilty and sentenced to life in prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his crime in particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: Killing. Like I say, torturing, killing, you know, the [? Wilhelm Tell ?] story, what I already mentioned. I don't want to take so much time, because I will repeat myself already.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to just--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I testified four times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Four times.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again-- four-- Hans-- Heinrich Kunerman. But this was in Frankfort, the trial later in early '80s, or late '70s. I don't recall. I have here a book from Erich Kulka, from the professor related at the Jerusalem university. And there, I recognized, from the pictures, also SS who was at the [NON-ENGLISH]. And but only I-- I-- I give up my testimony here at the consulate. Because I didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: You gave it here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I didn't go to the trial any more.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was he found guilty or innocent?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to read Erich's book. I did, but I don't know exactly what. It's here, all the names and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to just ask you a little about your daughter and then your grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. You know, like I said, my daughter was 14. She was set back one year in school because of her English. But in a half a year, she went back to her 9th grade. She finish- she went to the high school, Washington High School, on the 37, 38, diploma. Then she applied for college, was accepted. She was an A-one student here in San Francisco State. She got here her bachelor degree.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what? What did she study?

speaker: interviewee
text: Business administration. And her first job after she finished-- she got married in her college years. She has a daughter who is 20 years old-- who is now 23. I mean, she is in Arizona in Tempe in college studying anthropology. And my daughter has another son, Elliot, who attends the Hillel Brandeis school very good student. And my daughter, after getting her masters, she was offered a job in Wells Fargo Bank, her first job. And for her first salary-- how many years ago, 20 years ago-- was $25,000. First job. Then she succeeded. And she went to Bank of America, when she-- when she got doubled her salary. Then she went to, I think so, Schwab, where she was four or five years with double over. And now, she works for Intuit in Palo Alto. I don't know anymore how much she makes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are your children and grandchildren giving their children a Jewish education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My grandson is in the Hillel Brandeis. Although, my son-in-law is not Jewish. He's Catholic, born in San Francisco and raised in Catholic schools. But I think so, he knows more about Judaism than my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your son, Pavel?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son, the same. When he was in El Paso, he met a Mexican girl, married her. He was still in-- in the Air Force school. Then, when he finished and he went to Germany, he brought her over to San Francisco. She stayed with us. And they had a little girl, which is now a big girl and the mother of two, Caroline. And he was there two years in Frankfort, stationed. When he came back, he didn't want to take the job what they offered him in his profession, because he wanted to be in San Francisco. So we opened another retirement home in Daly City, and he was manager again, his wife. But he very good in computers. He divorced his first wife with the two children. And he has now his third marriage, which he's very happy-- a Chinese Soviet International.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your son has how many children?

speaker: interviewee
text: His-- from his first wife, he has two, Caroline and Steve. And recent marriage, he has one boy, 7, who is also raised Jewish and attends the Hillel Brandeis school and a very happy marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I just ask you-- all those dramatic episodes in your life, what are you doing now with all of that? Are you working somehow with the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am, you know, after I-- when I was working, I was very busy. I didn't have time. I just wanted to be successful not for my sake, so I can give a better life for my children. I can't complain about the life here. But for me, you know, until the retirement, it was work, work, work, work. But you know, work doesn't kill. And now, since I'm retired, I spend all my life for-- I mean, I'm very involved in the Holocaust. I was the founder of the organization, Tikvah, where I was the first president. I am still very involved with them. I was volunteered at the Montefiore Center for many years. I am still. And I am still involved with the Holocaust Center of Northern California.
